food_subgroup: Cooked prawn / shrimp dish — spiced curry preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for kadai prawn exists; FOODON_03302051 (shrimp dish) is the closest broader class. A more specific term for spiced Indian prawn preparations is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked spiced prawn dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific node for kadai-style Indian prawn curry. A0CKP (crustaceans, cooked) is the nearest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to kadai prawn as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage of ethnic prepared dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of kadai prawn in Singapore is approximately 250 g, comprising prawns in a thick spiced tomato-onion gravy, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn curry / cooked prawn preparation (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for prawn curry (cooked prawns in spiced tomato-onion gravy with oil). Kadai prawn is a dry-gravy variant of this preparation. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN prawn curry entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) to validate protein and fat ranges. The ICMR-NIN source is the primary anchor; USDA is used for cross-validation only. Serving size set at 250 g based on typical North Indian restaurant portion as documented in ICMR-NIN portion guidance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — prawn/shrimp curry analogous estimate; seafood-based curry reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kadai prawn specifically. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values for protein-dominant seafood and meat curry dishes in the range of 30–40, consistent with low-GI classification. The GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with cooked prawn and spiced tomato-based curry preparations, which are protein-rich and low in digestible carbohydrate. Carbohydrate per 100 g is 4.2 g; GL per 250 g serving = (35 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.7. This is a low GL. Estimation basis: high protein content, low carbohydrate density, tomato-onion base with minimal starch.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kadai prawn to small portions (≤150 g) and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 45–50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~3.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai prawn is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan, as its low carbohydrate content and high protein density support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~148 mg per 100 g) and sodium load from restaurant preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to ≤150 g and choose preparations with minimal added oil to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Prawn (also known as Karahi Prawn or Kadai Jhinga) is a popular North Indian seafood dish in which fresh prawns are stir-fried in a traditional iron wok called a kadai or karahi. The dish is characterised by a semi-dry, intensely spiced tomato-onion masala base made with whole and ground spices including coriander seeds, cumin, red chilli, ginger, garlic, and garam masala. Bell peppers (capsicum) are commonly added for texture and colour. The prawns are cooked until just tender, absorbing the bold, smoky flavours of the kadai-cooked sauce. The texture is firm and succulent with a thick, clinging gravy. Kadai Prawn is a protein-rich dish with moderate fat content, low carbohydrate density, and a low glycemic index, making it nutritionally suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations and warrants attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, kadai prawn is served across North Indian restaurants as a main course, typically paired with naan, roti, or steamed rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 112 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 4.2 g carbohydrate, with meaningful contributions of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iron from the prawn component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kadai prawn?
Is kadai prawn good for diabetics?
What spices are in kadai prawn?
Is kadai prawn high in sodium?
What is the difference between kadai prawn and prawn curry?
Can I eat kadai prawn if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN prawn curry data (2017 edition), which is the closest approved primary source for this North Indian prawn preparation. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for kadai prawn exists; values are adapted from the prawn curry category and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database seafood curry references. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact match for kadai prawn as a distinct dish exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) prawn curry entry; no exact kadai prawn entry exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases as of the access date. 2. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for kadai prawn has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 3. Sodium content will vary significantly between restaurant preparations; the value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant-style preparation with added salt and spice blends. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per ICMR-NIN and USDA references for this preparation type). 5. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (no pork, no alcohol); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. Gluten-free status assumes no wheat-based thickeners are used; restaurant preparations may vary. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology term for kadai prawn exists in current releases. 8. Serving size of 250 g is based on typical North Indian restaurant portion guidance from ICMR-NIN. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1.0 as it was not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source for prawn curry.

improvement_actions_taken: Canonical food_name simplified from 'Kadai Prawn (spicy)' to 'Kadai Prawn' with spicy variant captured in aliases.
Multilingual local_names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 for Indian cuisine in Singapore context.
Nutrient values sourced and adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) prawn curry entry as primary approved source.
GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database seafood curry references with full methodology documentation.
FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes assigned as closest_parent matches with gap notes; no codes invented.
All aliases_by_language entries verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Health context populated for all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
LLM natural language description written to exceed 200 characters with full clinical and nutritional context.
Added justification for vitamin_d_mcg = -1.0 in the 'notes' field to address G3 numeric hygiene failure.
Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to address G5 ontology honesty failure.
